name: Patti Page
abstract: Clara Ann Fowler (November 8, 1927 – January 1, 2013), better known by her stage name Patti Page, was an American singer. Primarily known for pop and country music, she was the top-charting female vocalist and best-selling female artist of the 1950s, selling over 100 million records during a six-decade-long career.

concerts.label: Patti Page: The 50th Anniversary Concert
concerts.venue: Isaac Stern Auditorium / Ronald O. Perelman Stage

about_quotes.speaker: Sherrie Maricle
about_quotes.evidence: he was the drummer with Josephine Baker, Patti Page and Frankie Laine
about_quotes.doc_title: Sherrie Maricle interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clearwater Beach, Florida, March 18, 2001 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Stanley Kay
about_quotes.evidence: I was. ... he was the original drummer of ‘How Much is that Doggie in the Window’ with Patti Page.
about_quotes.doc_title: Stanley Kay interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clearwater Beach, Florida, March 18, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Patricia Willard
about_quotes.evidence: I joined Patti Page... I did quite a bit of recording also with her

about_quotes.speaker: Tommy Newsom and Ross Tompkins
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] ...a Christmas album that was kind of interesting. There was five different singers — Vic Damone, Jack Jones, Joe Williams, Diane Carroll and Patti Page. [INTERVIEWEE] What a memory he’s got.
about_quotes.doc_title: Tommy Newsom and Ross Tompkins interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, September 3, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Anne Phillips
about_quotes.evidence: And of course there were a lot of those like Rosemary Clooney and Patti Page, who were really good singers but then they had to sing the stuff that was coming in now.
about_quotes.doc_title: Anne Phillips interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, February 24, 2025

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Cobb
about_quotes.evidence: Patti Page say, “You look like – you don’t look old enough to be a manger.” I say, “Yeah, well, I guess I don’t, but I am.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Cobb — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
